definition: Advanced Framework for Simulation, Integration, and Modeling. A government-developed simulation environment used for modeling complex military systems and engagement scenarios.
Architected Integration for Mission-Engineering Enablement. An internally focused capital software effort at MTSI that CIM-ARC leverages for secure data fabrics and APIs.

Classified Integration for Mission Engineering Alignment, Readiness, and Collaboration. The multi-year MTSI program delivering a classified mission engineering and integration architecture environment.
An organized inventory of data assets within an organization, including metadata, ownership, lineage, and classification. Enables discoverability and governance across the enterprise.
A logical grouping of related data assets owned by a specific business or mission area. CIM-ARC spans 14 defined data domains across the program.

An architecture and set of data services that provide consistent capabilities across a choice of endpoints spanning on-premises and cloud environments. Enables unified, policy-governed data access without physically moving data.
The life cycle of data — including its origins, movements, characteristics, and quality — tracked to ensure auditability and trust throughout the mission data pipeline.
A decentralized sociotechnical approach to sharing, accessing, and managing analytical data in complex, large-scale environments. Treats data as a product owned by domain teams.
A curated, governed, and trustworthy dataset or data service delivered by a domain team to internal or external consumers. CIM-ARC targets delivery of 200+ data products.
Development, Security, and Operations. A methodology that integrates security practices into the software development and operations lifecycle, ensuring security is built in rather than bolted on.
The communication framework that connects data flows across the lifecycle of a system — from design through operation — enabling traceability and informed decision-making at every stage.
A virtual representation of a physical system, updated with live data, that enables simulation, analysis, and prediction of system behavior across its lifecycle.
Department of Defense. The U.S. federal department responsible for national security. CIM-ARC operates at the DoD Secret classification level.
Intelligence Community. The network of U.S. government agencies involved in intelligence activities. CIM-ARC integrates IC data alongside DoD data for multi-domain awareness.
An open-source platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
MTSI's internal classified computing environment. CIM-ARC is initially hosted on LAVA before planned migration to Microsoft Azure Secret Cloud as capabilities expand.

Model-Based Systems Engineering. A systems engineering methodology centered on creating and exploiting domain models as the primary means of information exchange. CIM-ARC integrates MBSE artifacts via Cameo Systems Modeler.
Data that describes other data — including structure, content, quality, ownership, and lineage. Rich metadata is essential to the CIM-ARC data fabric for discoverability and governance.
Modeling, Simulation, and Analysis. The use of computational models to analyze system behavior and support decision-making. High-fidelity MS&A is a core CIM-ARC capability pillar.
Minimum Viable Product. Incremental delivery milestones within CIM-ARC that demonstrate working capability. Five MVPs are planned across the program: MVP-1 through MVP-5.
A graph database platform used within CIM-ARC to model and query complex relationships between data entities, systems, and mission elements.
An open-source metadata management platform used in CIM-ARC to catalog, govern, and surface data assets across the enterprise.
An open-source search and analytics engine used within CIM-ARC for indexing and querying large volumes of mission and operational data.
Program Management Review. A periodic review of program health covering schedule, cost, performance, and risk. The Digital PMR provides a real-time view of these indicators.

A security model that requires strict identity verification for every user and device attempting to access resources, regardless of network location. A foundational principle of the CIM-ARC security architecture.
